checks.a_analysis: The output faithfully represents the text found in the source document. The title, date, legend items, sources, and scale bar all have direct matches in the source PDF. The paragraph containing a list of county names consists of labels extracted from the map image via OCR. The image alt text and caption are AI-generated for accessibility, which is an expected change and not a defect. No fabricated content was found.
There are no phone numbers, email addresses, mailing addresses, or URLs present in the source document. Consequently, none appear in the output, and no fabricated contact information was introduced.

The source document is a single-page map. All textual components (title, date, legend, sources, and scale) are present in the output. The map itself is included as an image element. No sections or pages are missing.
There are no tables in the source document. The legend, which is visually a list of items with corresponding colors, has been correctly converted into a structured list in the output.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The output contains all significant text from the source. The title, date, legend entries, and source attribution are complete. The list of county names in the second paragraph is an extraction of the map's labels; while there are minor OCR artifacts (such as 'ALAMORAN' merging the 'ALAM' and 'ORAN' labels), the overall text content is preserved. There are no major omissions.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output matches the meaning and content of the source document. Reformatting line breaks (e.g., in the 'Sources' section or the scale bar) into paragraphs is an acceptable change for semantic structure. No wording changes that alter the meaning of the information were detected.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the source PDF and the JSON output. All text content from the source (headings, legend items, sources, scale) appears exactly once in the output. The paragraph containing the list of county names is an extraction of the labels found within the map image; while this text is technically present in the image, it is not duplicated as a text block in the output.

The heading hierarchy is sound. The main document title is assigned to the section title (H1). The date follows as an H2. Subsections 'Parcel Updates:' and 'Sources:' are correctly assigned as H3. There are no skipped levels or incorrect nestings.
The source document does not contain any tables. Consequently, no table structures are present in the output.

The output follows the logical reading order of the source document: starting with the title and date, followed by the map (image and extracted text), the legend, sources, and finally the scale information. This flow is consistent with the visual layout of the PDF.

The visual legend in the source document, which lists update periods and their statuses, has been correctly converted into a semantically appropriate unordered list in the JSON output. The items match the source content.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: The JSON contains 3 explicit heading elements and 1 section title, totaling 4 headings. The rendered HTML also contains 4 headings. The mismatch mentioned in the metadata (3 vs 4) is due to the programmatic count of 'type: heading' objects vs total H-tags in HTML, which includes the section title. This difference of 1 is well within the acceptable threshold of 3.

There are no links in the source PDF or the JSON output. The 4 links detected in the HTML metadata are consistent with navigational elements (skip links, ARIA-related links) added by the renderer, which is permitted under acceptable changes.

checks.a_analysis: The source document is a thematic map where the spatial organization of information is critical to its meaning. In the HTML output, the map has been entirely removed and replaced with a giant logo followed by a dense text list of county names. This fundamentally alters the visual organization of the content, making it impossible for a user to find information in a similar manner to the source.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source document features a central map of North Carolina with color-coded counties representing project status.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output replaces the map image with a text block: 'HYDE DARE CART BEAU COLU BLAD ROBE SAMP...'
checks.b_evidence_location: Main document body, following the title and date.

checks.a_analysis: There are no tables in the source document to evaluate.

The most meaningful image in the source—the color-coded map of North Carolina—is missing from the HTML output. Additionally, the legend 'Parcel Updates' is missing the colored swatches that correlate to the map's data, rendering the legend meaningless as a visual guide.
checks.b_evidence_source: A color-coded map of North Carolina and colored square swatches next to labels in the 'Parcel Updates' legend.
checks.b_evidence_output: The map is absent, and the legend is rendered as a plain text bulleted list without color indicators.
checks.b_evidence_location: Main body (map) and 'Parcel Updates' section (legend swatches).

checks.a_analysis: No visual blocks or content sections appear to be duplicated in the HTML output.

The visual quality is significantly degraded because the core functionality of the document (the map and its color-coded legend) is lost. Furthermore, the NCGICC logos are rendered at a disproportionately large scale, dominating the page and creating an unbalanced and unprofessional appearance compared to the source PDF.
checks.b_evidence_source: A professionally formatted document with a map, clear legend, and appropriately sized logos.
checks.b_evidence_output: Oversized logos, missing legend colors, and a text dump replacing the primary data visualization.
